## Referee report

### Executive verdict

The submitted `answer.tex` is **not a complete rigorous solution**. Conditional on the stated “Kellerer” lemma, the rest of the proof is essentially correct; however, that lemma is exactly the hard part of the problem for \(0\le p\le 1/3\), and `answer.tex` neither proves it nor gives a sufficiently precise, validated citation and derivation from the cited literature. This is a fatal gap.

### LaTeX contract audit

I independently compiled the provided `answer.tex` with `pdflatex`. After the normal second run, it compiled successfully and produced a 3-page PDF. The source uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses `fullpage`, and I found no forbidden margin/layout package, line-spacing change, or explicit in-document font-size command. Thus I do **not** find a First Proof LaTeX contract violation.

### Literature/citation audit

Springer confirms the cited Kellerer paper exists with the stated title, venue, volume, pages, and DOI metadata. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF00532495)) But the accessible PDF text shows that the paper defines an “analogous region” as a measurable set \(A\) satisfying \(\mu(A)=\theta\) for every measure in a family, under atomlessness assumptions. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/BF00532495.pdf)) Its relevant-looking finite theorem, Satz 4, concerns finite families of atomless measures and existence of analogous regions of the form \(\{a\in G\}\), not the Bernoulli product-measure halfspace inequality stated in `answer.tex`. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/BF00532495.pdf)) A text search of the PDF found no occurrence of “Bernoulli.” ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/BF00532495.pdf))

This does **not** prove the claimed Kellerer lemma is false, but it means the submitted proof has not validated that the cited theorem actually implies the stated finite weighted Bernoulli inequality. A rigorous solution would need either:

1. a self-contained proof of the lemma; or  
2. a precise theorem number/statement from the literature, plus a careful proof that its hypotheses apply and that it specializes to the atomic Bernoulli weighted-sum setting, including treatment of the weak inequality at the threshold.

### Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

#### Problem statement and interpretation

The author explicitly adopts the universal interpretation: determine all \(p\in[0,1]\) for which the inequality holds for every \(m\) and every nonnegative probability vector \(w\). This is a reasonable interpretation of the problem, and the ambiguity is recorded.

#### Claimed answer

The claimed set
\[
[0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}
\]
is plausible and consistent with the later counterexamples. No issue here except that the sufficiency on \([0,1/3]\) is not proved.

#### Kellerer lemma

This is the fatal gap. The lemma states exactly:
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\quad\text{for all weights and }0\le p\le 1/3.
\]
That is precisely the main nontrivial positive direction of the problem. The answer gives no proof.

The following explanatory sentence is not enough:

> “For reference, this is the specialization to finite weighted sums of Kellerer’s ‘analogue domains’ theorem…”

No specialization is shown. In particular, the answer does not explain:

- what atomless measure space from Kellerer’s theorem is being used;
- how the finite atomic Bernoulli product measure is represented;
- how the weighted halfspace \(\{\sum w_i v_i\ge p\}\) appears as, contains, or is measured by a Kellerer analogous region;
- where the constant \(1/3\) enters from Kellerer’s paper;
- why weak threshold \(\ge p\), rather than \(>p\), is preserved under any limiting or embedding argument.

Thus Lemma 1 remains an unproved essential lemma.

#### Sufficiency for \(0\le p\le 1/3\)

This part is valid only if the Kellerer lemma is accepted. Since the lemma is not established, the sufficiency proof for the whole interval \([0,1/3]\) is incomplete.

The separate endpoint \(p=0\) is correct: the weighted sum is nonnegative, so the event has probability \(1\).

#### Sufficiency for \(p=1/2\)

This argument is correct. For \(p=1/2\), the vector \((1-v_i)_i\) has the same joint distribution as \((v_i)_i\), so
\[
X=\sum_i w_i v_i
\quad\text{and}\quad
1-X=\sum_i w_i(1-v_i)
\]
have the same distribution. Therefore
\[
\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2],
\]
and hence
\[
\Pr[X\ge 1/2]\ge 1/2.
\]
No gap here.

#### Sufficiency for \(p=1\)

Correct. Bernoulli\((1)\) variables equal \(1\) almost surely, so the weighted sum is \(1\) almost surely.

#### Necessity for \(1/3<p<1/2\)

Correct. With \(m=3\) and equal weights,
\[
\frac{v_1+v_2+v_3}{3}\ge p
\]
requires at least two successes. The probability is
\[
3p^2(1-p)+p^3=3p^2-2p^3.
\]
The difference from \(p\) is
\[
3p^2-2p^3-p=p(1-p)(2p-1)<0
\]
for \(1/3<p<1/2\). I also cross-checked this exact calculation computationally.

#### Necessity for \(1/2<p<1\)

Correct. With \(m=2\) and equal weights, the event requires two successes, so its probability is \(p^2<p\). This excludes all \(p\in(1/2,1)\).

#### Boundary points

The boundary handling is correct conditional on the Kellerer lemma:

- \(p=0\): trivial.
- \(p=1/3\): included only via the unproved Kellerer lemma.
- \(p=1/2\): proved by symmetry.
- \(p=1\): trivial.

The only problematic boundary is \(p=1/3\), because it depends on the unproved positive theorem.

### Additional clarity issue

The phrase

> “the lower \(p\)-biased measure of a weighted halfspace whose threshold is its \(p\)-mean is \(p\)”

is unclear. If “lower” means “infimum over all weight vectors,” this should be explicitly defined. If read literally as saying every such halfspace has measure exactly \(p\), it is false; many weights give strictly larger probability.

### Required repair

To make the proof rigorous, the author must replace the black-box Kellerer lemma by one of the following:

1. a complete proof of the finite weighted Bernoulli inequality for \(0\le p\le1/3\); or  
2. a precise, verifiable citation to a theorem that states exactly this inequality, with theorem number/page and all hypotheses; or  
3. a detailed derivation from Kellerer’s analogous-region theorem, including all translations from atomless analogous regions to Bernoulli weighted sums.

Without this, the main sufficiency interval is unsupported.

### Final assessment

The negative-direction counterexamples and the \(p=1/2,1\) arguments are sound. The LaTeX contract is satisfied. But the solution is not answer-ready because the main theorem for \(0\le p\le1/3\) is only asserted, not proved or adequately validated.

## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Overall verdict

The revised draft is **not answer-ready**. It is mathematically more honest than the previous version: it removes the unsupported Kellerer black-box assertion and correctly proves some additional positive cases, notably all reciprocal points \(p=1/k\). However, it explicitly leaves the central small-\(p\) lemma unresolved. Since that lemma is exactly the sufficiency of the whole interval \([0,1/3]\), the draft remains a partial solution, not a complete characterization.

### LaTeX contract audit

I compiled the revised `answer.tex` with `pdflatex`. After a second run to resolve references, it compiled successfully and produced a 3-page PDF. The source uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses `fullpage`, and I found no forbidden margin/layout changes, line-spacing changes, or in-document font-size changes. Thus I find **no First Proof LaTeX contract violation**.

### Literature/citation audit

The revised `answer.tex` no longer invokes Kellerer or any other external theorem. The separate `references.bib` still contains the Kellerer entry, and Springer metadata confirms the paper exists with the listed title, journal, volume, pages, and DOI information. But since `answer.tex` does not cite or use that result, there is no remaining literature-based proof obligation inside the submitted solution. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF00532495))

### Response to previous concerns

- **Unsupported Kellerer black box:** addressed only by removal. The draft no longer falsely treats Kellerer’s theorem as proving the needed inequality.
- **Missing proof of the small-\(p\) interval:** still unresolved. This is the fatal remaining gap.
- **Unclear “lower \(p\)-biased measure” language:** removed.
- **Endpoint and counterexample arguments:** retained and remain valid.
- **Newly added reciprocal proof:** valid and useful, but insufficient for the full interval \([0,1/3]\).

### Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

#### Problem statement and interpretation

The universal interpretation is clearly stated: determine those \(p\in[0,1]\) for which the inequality holds for every finite nonnegative probability vector \(w\). This is appropriate.

#### “The expected complete answer”

The phrase “expected complete answer” is already a warning sign. The draft announces
\[
[0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\},
\]
but does not prove the full positive interval. A final solution should state and prove the answer, not merely identify it as expected.

#### Proposition: positive values proved here

The proof for \(p=0\) is correct.

The proof for \(p=1/k\) is correct. For each coloring outcome,
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k W_j=1,
\]
so at least one \(W_j\ge 1/k\). By symmetry,
\[
k\Pr[W_1\ge 1/k]\ge 1.
\]
The indicators of membership in color \(1\) are independent Bernoulli\((1/k)\), so this gives exactly the desired inequality for \(p=1/k\). This rigorously proves \(p=1/3\) and all reciprocal points below it.

The proof for \(p=1/2\) is correct: \(X\) and \(1-X\) have the same distribution, so
\[
\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2],
\]
which implies \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\).

The proof for \(p=1\) is correct.

#### Proposition: values that cannot work

The counterexample for \(1/3<p<1/2\) is correct. With three equal weights, the event requires at least two successes, so its probability is
\[
3p^2(1-p)+p^3=3p^2-2p^3.
\]
The difference from \(p\) is
\[
3p^2-2p^3-p=p(1-p)(2p-1)<0
\]
on \(1/3<p<1/2\).

The counterexample for \(1/2<p<1\) is also correct. With two equal weights, the event requires two successes, so the probability is \(p^2<p\).

Thus the negative direction is complete.

#### Remaining open issues

This section is fatal for answer-readiness. The draft explicitly states that the following lemma remains missing:
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\quad\text{for }0\le p\le1/3.
\]
This lemma is precisely the missing sufficiency statement for all non-reciprocal \(p\in(0,1/3)\). Without it, the announced answer is not proved.

The normalization using \(a_i=w_i/p\) and \(L=1/p\) is algebraically correct in the case \(w_i<p\). Strictly, to call it an equivalent reduction for the whole lemma, the draft should also note that if some \(w_i\ge p\), then the event contains \(\{v_i=1\}\), which already has probability \(p\). This is a minor exposition gap, not the main issue.

The deterministic rational-subset counterexample is correct:
\[
\binom73/\binom{10}3=35/120<3/10.
\]
But this is only an explanation of a failed approach, not progress toward the missing proof.

### Final assessment

The revised draft successfully proves:

\[
p=0,\quad p=1/k\ (k\ge3),\quad p=1/2,\quad p=1,
\]
and successfully disproves all
\[
p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1).
\]

It does **not** prove the required inequality for non-reciprocal \(p\in(0,1/3)\). Since the draft explicitly contains a “Remaining open issues” section and an unproved essential lemma, it is not a complete rigorous solution of the stated problem.

## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Executive verdict

The revised draft is still **not answer-ready**. It is candidly partial: it explicitly leaves the central small-\(p\) lemma unresolved. The newly added positive family \(p=2/b\), \(b\ge6\), appears correct, and the convex-hull/down-set reformulation is essentially correct, though it would benefit from a few missing explanatory details. But the proof still does **not** establish the claimed full answer
\[
[0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}.
\]

### LaTeX contract audit

I compiled the submitted `answer.tex` twice with `pdflatex`. It compiled successfully and produced a 4-page PDF. The file uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses `fullpage`, and I found no forbidden margin/layout changes, line-spacing changes, or in-document font-size changes. Thus I find **no First Proof LaTeX contract violation**.

### Literature/citation audit

`answer.tex` itself does not cite or use any external theorem, and it contains no bibliography. Therefore the previous unsupported Kellerer black-box issue has been avoided rather than solved.

The supplied `references.bib` is unused. Its Kellerer metadata is corroborated by Springer’s page for “Zur Existenz analoger Bereiche,” which lists Hans G. Kellerer, volume 1, pages 240–246, 1963, and DOI `10.1007/BF00532495`. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF00532495)) The Fokkink–Meester–Pelekis metadata is also corroborated by the ALEA PDF, which lists volume 20, pages 153–165, 2023, and DOI `10.30757/ALEA.v20-07`. ([alea.impa.br](https://alea.impa.br/articles/v20/20-07.pdf)) The Pinelis entry in `references.bib` appears to have a DOI mismatch if it is later used: a Michigan Tech repository page lists the publisher’s-version DOI as `10.1214/16-EJP4474`, whereas the submitted `.bib` gives `10.1214/16-EJP6`. ([digitalcommons.mtu.edu](https://digitalcommons.mtu.edu/math-fp/1)) This does not affect the mathematical proof, since no bibliography is included in `answer.tex`.

### Response to previous concerns

- **Unsupported Kellerer theorem:** addressed by removal. The draft no longer relies on Kellerer.
- **Full small-\(p\) sufficiency:** still unresolved. This remains the fatal gap.
- **Reciprocal points \(p=1/k\):** still correctly proved.
- **New family \(p=2/b\), \(b\ge6\):** newly added; the proof appears valid.
- **Convex-hull/down-set reformulation:** newly added; essentially correct, but not a substitute for the missing lemma.
- **Negative counterexamples:** remain correct and complete.
- **“Remaining open issues” section:** still present, and it explicitly identifies an unproved essential lemma.

### Section-by-section mathematical audit

#### Problem statement and interpretation

The universal interpretation is clearly stated: determine those \(p\in[0,1]\) for which the inequality holds for every finite probability vector \(w\). This addresses the ambiguity of the original problem.

However, the phrase “the only possible complete answer is” is not backed by a full proof. The draft proves that no \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\) can work, but it still does not prove all \(p\in[0,1/3]\) work.

#### Proposition: positive values proved here

The cases \(p=0\) and \(p=1\) are immediate and correct.

The coloring proof for reciprocal points \(p=1/k\), \(k\ge3\), remains correct. For each coloring, at least one color class has total weight at least \(1/k\), and by symmetry the first color has this property with probability at least \(1/k\). Membership in color 1 gives independent Bernoulli\((1/k)\) indicators.

The new pair lemma for \(p=2/b\), \(b\ge6\), appears correct. The deletion argument is sound:

- scaling to \(z_i=bx_i/2\) is correct;
- if the smallest plus largest is at least 1, the largest forms good pairs with all remaining elements;
- otherwise, the estimate forcing \(h>\frac{s-2}{2}\) is valid;
- after deleting the smallest and largest, the residual sum remains \(>(s-2)/2\);
- the counted pairs are distinct because each deletion step counts pairs involving a maximum element that is then removed.

The final counting formula also checks out for both even and odd \(b\). A minor improvement would be to explicitly state why pairs counted in different deletion stages are distinct, but this is not a serious gap.

The deduction from the pair lemma to the Bernoulli statement for \(p=2/b\) is correct. Coloring into \(b\) colors and looking at colors 1 and 2 selects each original index independently with probability \(2/b\).

The \(p=1/2\) symmetry argument is correct.

#### Proposition: values that cannot work

This section remains correct.

For \(1/3<p<1/2\), the equal-weight \(m=3\) example gives
\[
\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(3,p)\ge2]=3p^2-2p^3,
\]
and
\[
3p^2-2p^3-p=p(1-p)(2p-1)<0.
\]

For \(1/2<p<1\), the equal-weight \(m=2\) example gives probability \(p^2<p\). Thus the negative direction is complete.

#### Equivalent form of the remaining small-\(p\) lemma

The normalization
\[
a_i=w_i/p,\qquad L=1/p
\]
is correct in the nontrivial case \(w_i<p\). The draft now explicitly notes that if some \(w_i\ge p\), the result is immediate from the event \(\{v_i=1\}\), which addresses a minor exposition gap from the previous version.

The convex-hull/down-set equivalence is essentially correct. The first direction is valid: if the Bernoulli inequality fails for weights \(w\), then the down-set
\[
\mathcal D=\{A:w(A)<p\}
\]
has \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p\), and a distribution on \(\mathcal D\) with coordinate marginals \(p\) would force
\[
p=\mathbb E w(A)<p,
\]
a contradiction.

The converse is also basically correct, but the writeup omits a few details that should be added for full rigor:

1. One should explicitly note that the convex hull of a down-set is coordinatewise down-closed.
2. Therefore, if \((p,\ldots,p)\notin\operatorname{conv}\{\mathbf 1_A:A\in\mathcal D\}\), then the convex hull is disjoint from \((p,\ldots,p)+\mathbb R_{\ge0}^m\).
3. Separating these two convex sets yields a nonnegative separating vector \(c\), because otherwise the infimum over the upper orthant would be \(-\infty\).
4. The notation
   \[
   \Pr[\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p]
   \]
   should specify that \(A\) is a \(p\)-biased random subset.

These are polish/rigor issues in the reformulation, not the main fatal gap.

#### Remaining open issues

This section is fatal for answer-readiness. The draft explicitly says:

> “The essential gap is still Lemma~\ref{lem:missing} for the non-covered values of \(p\in(0,1/3)\).”

That lemma is exactly the missing sufficiency statement for the full interval \(0\le p\le1/3\). Since the problem asks for a complete characterization, a solution with this unresolved lemma cannot be accepted as complete.

### Final assessment

The revised draft proves more than the previous one: it now establishes the positive cases
\[
p=0,\quad p=1/k\ (k\ge3),\quad p=2/b\ (b\ge6),\quad p=1/2,\quad p=1,
\]
and it still correctly excludes all
\[
p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1).
\]

However, it does not prove the desired inequality for the remaining non-covered values of \(p\in(0,1/3)\). Since `answer.tex` explicitly contains a remaining essential open issue, it is not a complete rigorous solution.

## Referee report

### Overall verdict

The submission is **not answer-ready**. The proposed final classification is evidently intended to be  
\[
[0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\},
\]
but `answer.tex` explicitly does **not** prove the sufficiency of the full interval \(0\le p\le 1/3\). It introduces this as Lemma 3 / “small-\(p\) lemma” and later states that it remains unresolved. That is a fatal gap for the stated problem.

### LaTeX contract check

I recreated and compiled the supplied `answer.tex` with `pdflatex` twice.

- Document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- `fullpage` is used, and no prohibited layout packages or manual margin changes appear.
- No prohibited line-spacing changes or explicit in-document font-size commands appear.
- Compilation succeeds.
- The resulting PDF is 5 pages, within the 12-page limit.

So I found **no First Proof LaTeX contract violation**. The failure is mathematical completeness, not formatting.

### Literature / reference check

The supplied `references.bib` is **not used** in `answer.tex`: there are no `\cite`, `\bibliography`, or stated external theorems. Thus none of the listed literature can repair the missing proof unless the paper explicitly imports and proves the needed implication.

I checked the listed references externally. Fokkink–Meester–Pelekis is indeed a 2023 ALEA paper on optimizing tail probabilities of convex combinations of iid Bernoulli variables, but its abstract states it proves the relevant optimization conjecture only for a range of parameters, and the paper itself discusses remaining gaps; `answer.tex` does not derive the small-\(p\) lemma from it. ([alea.impa.br](https://alea.impa.br/articles/v20/20-07.pdf)) Kellerer’s 1963 bibliographic metadata matches the supplied reference, but no Kellerer theorem is stated or applied in the submitted proof. ([ftp.math.utah.edu](https://ftp.math.utah.edu/pub/tex/bib/z-wahrscheinlichkeits-theor-verw-geb.html)) The Pinelis, Hoeffding, and León–Perron references are real and broadly related to tail inequalities / Bernoulli sums, but again they are not invoked in `answer.tex` and do not by themselves constitute a proof of the missing lemma. ([digitalcommons.mtu.edu](https://digitalcommons.mtu.edu/math-fp/1))

### Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

#### Problem statement and interpretation

The document adopts the universal interpretation: determine those \(p\in[0,1]\) for which the inequality holds for every finite nonnegative probability vector \(w\). This is a reasonable interpretation and is explicitly recorded.

However, the same paragraph says:

> “The remaining unresolved assertion is the sufficiency of the full interval \(0\le p\le1/3\).”

This alone makes the document incomplete as a solution to “For which values of \(p\)?” The answer does not establish the claimed full set.

#### Proposition: positive values

The cases \(p=0\) and \(p=1\) are correctly described as immediate.

The coloring proof for \(p=1/k\), \(k\ge3\), is valid. For each coloring, at least one color class has weight at least \(1/k\); averaging over colors gives  
\[
\Pr[W_1\ge1/k]\ge 1/k.
\]
Color membership in a fixed color is indeed independent Bernoulli\((1/k)\), so this proves the desired inequality for these reciprocal points.

#### Pair lemma for \(p=2/b\)

The deterministic pair lemma appears essentially correct. The scaling \(z_i=bx_i/2\) is appropriate, and the deletion/counting argument is plausible and, after filling in minor endpoint details, works.

Minor presentation issues:

- The stopping/counting argument uses an ellipsis in the finite sum. This is not fatal, but a polished proof should state explicitly what the last term is in the even and odd cases.
- The proof says “if the procedure never stops before the final one or two elements”; formally, the algorithm should specify what happens when \(s=1\) or \(s=2\).
- The computation of the difference from \(b-1\) is stated uniformly for \(b=2r\) and \(b=2r+1\); it is correct, but a strict proof should spell out the two cases or define the relevant range of \(t\) more carefully.

These are not the main obstruction. I did not find a counterexample to the pair lemma, and numerical sanity checks for small \(b\) were consistent with the stated bound.

The subsequent coloring argument for \(p=2/b\) is valid: by symmetry, the probability that the fixed pair of colors \(\{1,2\}\) is good is at least
\[
\frac{b-1}{\binom b2}=\frac2b,
\]
and membership in colors \(1\) or \(2\) gives independent Bernoulli\((2/b)\) selection.

#### Case \(p=1/2\)

The symmetry proof is correct. For \(p=1/2\), \(v_i\) and \(1-v_i\) have the same law, so \(X\) and \(1-X\) have the same law. Hence
\[
\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2],
\]
and therefore \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\), including the possible atom at \(1/2\).

#### Multiplicative closure

The closure proposition
\[
p,q\in\mathcal G\implies pq\in\mathcal G
\]
is correct. The construction \(v_i=x_i y_i\) gives independent Bernoulli\((pq)\) variables. Conditional on \(T=\sum_i w_i x_i\ge p\), the normalized weights \(w_i x_i/T\) form a probability vector, and the \(q\)-inequality applies. This proves the closure statement.

No serious gap found here.

#### Corollary reducing to \([1/6,1/3]\)

The corollary is correct as a conditional reduction. For every \(0<p\le1/3\), one can choose \(n\) such that \(2^n p\in[1/6,1/3]\), and then use multiplicative closure with \(1/2\).

But this is only conditional. The base interval \([1/6,1/3]\) is not proved.

#### Negative examples

The exclusion of \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\) using \(m=3\), equal weights, is correct:
\[
\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(3,p)\ge2]=3p^2-2p^3,
\]
and
\[
3p^2-2p^3-p=p(1-p)(2p-1)<0
\]
for \(1/3<p<1/2\).

The exclusion of \(p\in(1/2,1)\) using \(m=2\), equal weights, is also correct: the event requires two successes and has probability \(p^2<p\).

Thus the negative direction is complete for the proposed classification.

#### Small-\(p\) lemma

This is the fatal missing part. The lemma

\[
0\le p\le1/3 \implies 
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]

is exactly the remaining sufficiency statement for the interval \([0,1/3]\). It is **not proved**.

The normalization \(a_i=w_i/p\), \(L=1/p\), is correct for \(p>0\) and under the assumption \(w_i<p\). The text should explicitly exclude \(p=0\) before dividing by \(p\), though \(p=0\) is trivial. This is a minor clarity issue, not the main gap.

The statement that the reciprocal coloring proof covers integral \(L\) is correct, but nonintegral \(L\ge3\) remains open in the submission.

#### Convex-hull reformulation

The convex-hull equivalence is mostly correct.

Forward direction: if the convex-hull statement holds and the Bernoulli inequality fails, the down-set
\[
\mathcal D=\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\}
\]
has \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p\), and a distribution on \(\mathcal D\) with coordinate marginals \(p\) gives the contradiction
\[
p=\sum_i p w_i=\mathbb E w(A)<p.
\]
This is valid.

Reverse direction: the proof that \(P(\mathcal D)\) is coordinatewise down-closed is valid via thinning. The separation argument is also essentially valid: because \(P(\mathcal D)\) is compact and disjoint from the closed upper orthant \((p,\ldots,p)+\mathbb R_{\ge0}^m\), strong separation gives a nonzero separating vector. The argument that the vector must be nonnegative is correct.

Minor issues:

- The phrase “assume the convex-hull statement fails for a down-set \(\mathcal D\)” should explicitly include both assumptions:
  \[
  \mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p
  \quad\text{and}\quad
  p\mathbf 1\notin P(\mathcal D).
  \]
- Empty down-sets should be excluded or handled when defining \(\operatorname{conv}\varnothing\). Under the antecedent \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p\), this is automatic, but the partition-function formulation should be precise.

These are fixable exposition issues. The bigger issue is that this reformulation is equivalent to the missing lemma; it is not a proof of it.

#### Partition-function formulation

The displayed partition-function implication is indeed the contrapositive of the convex-hull statement after substituting
\[
\lambda=\frac{p}{1-p}.
\]
The algebra
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal D)
=(1+\lambda)^{-m}\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\lambda^{|A|}
\]
is correct.

But again, this is only an equivalent target. It does not prove the inequality.

#### Remaining open issues

This section explicitly states:

> “The essential gap is still Lemma~\ref{lem:missing}.”

and

> “it does not yet prove the missing base interval.”

This is a direct admission that the solution is incomplete. Under the requested grading rule, a submission with unresolved essential lemmas or “Remaining open issues” cannot be answer-ready.

### Summary of blocking issues

## Mathematical audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The universal interpretation is clear and faithful: determine those \(p\in[0,1]\) for which the inequality holds for every finite nonnegative probability vector. This is appropriate.

However, the same section explicitly says the sufficiency of \(0\le p\le1/3\) is unresolved. That is fatal for a complete solution.

### Positive values

The proof for \(p=0\) and \(p=1\) is correct.

The coloring proof for \(p=1/k\), \(k\ge3\), is correct. For every coloring, at least one color class has weight at least \(1/k\), and symmetry gives
\[
\Pr[W_1\ge 1/k]\ge 1/k.
\]
Color membership in a fixed color is independent Bernoulli\((1/k)\), so this proves the desired inequality.

The proof for \(p=2/b\), \(b\ge6\), rests on Lemma 1. I checked the logic of the deletion/counting proof and found it essentially valid. The last counting step should ideally spell out the even and odd cases, but I did not find a mathematical error.

The \(p=1/2\) symmetry argument is correct: \(X\) and \(1-X\) have the same distribution, so
\[
\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2],
\]
which implies \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\).

### Multiplicative closure

The closure proposition
\[
p,q\in\mathcal G\implies pq\in\mathcal G
\]
is correct. The product construction \(v_i=x_i y_i\) gives iid Bernoulli\((pq)\) variables, and the conditioning/normalization argument is valid.

### Base interval corollary

The corollary is correct as a conditional statement: if one proves the inequality for all \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), then multiplicative closure with \(1/2\) gives all \(p\in[0,1/3]\).

But it remains conditional. The base interval is not proved.

### Negative examples

The exclusion of \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\) using three equal weights is correct:
\[
\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(3,p)\ge2]=3p^2-2p^3<p.
\]

The exclusion of \(p\in(1/2,1)\) using two equal weights is also correct:
\[
\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(2,p)=2]=p^2<p.
\]

So the negative direction is complete.

### Complement formulation

The complement formulation is correct. Since
\[
\sum_i w_i v_i = 1-\sum_i w_i(1-v_i),
\]
the event \(\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\) is equivalent to
\[
\sum_i w_i\eta_i\le 1-p=q.
\]
Thus the desired inequality is equivalent to
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right]\le q.
\]

### Pivot inequality

The pivot equivalence is valid.

For \(a=0\), the pivot inequality gives
\[
U\ge \frac{p}{1-p}(1-U),
\]
hence \(U\ge p\), recovering the small-\(p\) lemma.

Conversely, assuming the small-\(p\) lemma and adding an extra weight \(a\), conditioning on the extra Bernoulli variable gives
\[
p\,\Pr[Y\ge p-a]+(1-p)\Pr[Y\ge p]\ge p.
\]
Rearranging indeed yields
\[
\Pr[Y\ge p]\ge \frac{p}{1-p}\Pr[Y<p-a].
\]

This is a correct equivalence, but it is not a proof of the missing lemma.

### Pivot-plus-dust construction

The sharpness construction is correct. With
\[
a=p-\varepsilon,\qquad
Y_N=\frac{1-a}{N}\operatorname{Bin}(N,p),
\]
we have
\[
Y_N\to p(1-a)=p(1-p+\varepsilon)
\]
in probability, and
\[
\varepsilon<p(1-p+\varepsilon)<p.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[Y_N\ge\varepsilon]\to1,\qquad
\Pr[Y_N\ge p]\to0,
\]
and the success probability tends to \(p\).

This correctly shows asymptotic sharpness. It does not establish the lower bound.

### Convex-hull formulation

The convex-hull equivalence is mostly correct.

Forward direction: valid.

Reverse direction: the overall separation argument is valid in principle, but the proof is slightly compressed. In particular, the coordinatewise down-closed property of \(P(\mathcal D)\) is asserted without proof. This can be filled by the thinning argument from the previous draft, but in the revised text it is omitted.

This omission is minor compared with the main gap, because this section is only an equivalent reformulation of the missing lemma.

### Partition-function formulation

The partition-function statement
\[
p\mathbf1\notin P(\mathcal D)
\Longrightarrow
\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\lambda^{|A|}
\le (1+\lambda)^{m-1}
\]
with \(\lambda=p/(1-p)\) is algebraically equivalent to the convex-hull formulation. The conversion using
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal D)=(1+\lambda)^{-m}\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\lambda^{|A|}
\]
is correct.

Again, this is not a proof.

The revision improves the exposition by adding correct equivalent formulations and a useful sharpness example. But it still does **not** solve the problem. The central sufficiency statement for \(0\le p\le1/3\) remains unproved and is explicitly listed as an open issue.

Therefore the revised `answer.tex` is not a complete rigorous solution.

## New coalescence section

The local coalescence formula is essentially correct. For \(q=1-p\), merging two weights \(x\le y\) changes the strict upper-tail probability by
\[
pq\left(
\Pr[q-x-y<Z\le q-y]
-
\Pr[q-x<Z\le q]
\right),
\]
with the displayed endpoint choices matching the strict inequality convention.

However, the proposed coalescence assertion (C) is false.

Take
\[
q=0.7,\qquad p=0.3,\qquad w_1=w_2=w_3=w_4=\frac14.
\]
Here all weights satisfy \(w_i=0.25<p=0.3\), so this is even inside the “nontrivial cap” regime mentioned by the author.

For the original vector,
\[
\Phi_q(w)
=\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(4,q)\ge3]
=4q^3(1-q)+q^4.
\]
At \(q=0.7\), this equals
\[
4(0.7)^3(0.3)+(0.7)^4=0.6517.
\]

If any pair is coalesced, the new vector is
\[
\left(\frac12,\frac14,\frac14\right).
\]
The event
\[
\frac12\eta_0+\frac14\eta_1+\frac14\eta_2>0.7
\]
occurs exactly when the merged coordinate succeeds and at least one of the two remaining coordinates succeeds. Its probability is
\[
q\bigl(1-(1-q)^2\bigr)
=0.7(1-0.3^2)=0.637.
\]
Thus every possible coalescence strictly decreases \(\Phi_q\):
\[
0.637-0.6517=-0.0147<0.
\]
So assertion (C), as stated, fails.

This does not make the main theorem false; it only invalidates the proposed coalescence route and the claim that computations found no capped counterexample.

There is also a small endpoint inaccuracy: the text says the one-coordinate vector has \(\Phi_q=q\). This is true for \(q<1\), but not for \(q=1\), where the strict event \(\eta>1\) has probability \(0\). Since \(p=0\) is trivial, this is minor, but the statement should be qualified.

The cases \(p=0,1\) are immediate. The \(p=1/2\) symmetry argument is correct: for Bernoulli\((1/2)\), \(X\) and \(1-X\) have the same distribution, hence \(\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2]\), giving \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\).

#### Multiplicative closure and topological closure

The multiplicative closure proposition is valid. The construction \(v_i=x_i y_i\), with \(x_i\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(p)\), \(y_i\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(q)\), correctly gives Bernoulli\((pq)\) variables, and the conditional normalization step is justified on the event \(T=\sum_i w_i x_i\ge p>0\).

The closedness proof for \(\mathcal G\) is also valid. The only delicate point is the moving threshold, but the proof handles it by separating the finite set of subset sums below/above \(p\). The two one-sided subsequence arguments correctly imply \(F(p)\ge p\) for every fixed finite weight vector.

The base-interval corollary is valid: if all \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\) were known, multiplicative closure with \(1/2\in\mathcal G\) would imply all \(p\in(0,1/3]\).

The counterexamples are correct and complete for the claimed negative intervals. For \(1/3<p<1/2\), three equal weights force at least two successes, giving probability \(3p^2-2p^3<p\). For \(1/2<p<1\), two equal weights force two successes, giving probability \(p^2<p\).

Thus the negative direction is fully proved.

The sharpness examples are correct. The “large coordinate plus small remainder” example gives exact equality. The pivot-plus-dust construction correctly tends to probability \(p\), because the dust contribution converges in probability to a value strictly between \(\varepsilon\) and \(p\).

Since \(X\) and \(1-X\) have the same distribution, \(\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2]\), hence \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\). #### Deterministic coloring obstruction The \(a/b=3/10\) example is correct: seven entries \(1/7\) and three entries \(0\) give only \(\binom73=35\) heavy triples, below the target \(\binom92=36\). This correctly shows that the deterministic fixed-size subset strengthening cannot prove all rational cases. #### Multiplicative closure Proposition 4 is valid. The product representation \(v_i=x_i y_i\) gives Bernoulli\((pq)\) variables, and conditioning on the \(x_i\)’s with \(T=\sum w_i x_i\ge p\) correctly invokes the \(q\)-inequality on normalized weights. #### Closedness of \(\mathcal G\) The proof that \(\mathcal G\) is closed is correct. For a fixed finite weight vector, only finitely many subset-sum thresholds occur, so along one-sided subsequences the event stabilizes to either \(\{w(A)\ge p\}\) or \(\{w(A)>p\}\), and continuity of the finite product measure gives the desired conclusion. #### Reduction to \([1/6,1/3]\) Corollary 5 follows from multiplicative closure and \(1/2\in\mathcal G\). This is correct, conditional on having proved the base interval. #### Negative direction Proposition 6 is correct. - For \(1/3q\). The two-tail pivot inequality is correctly equivalent to Conjecture 1. The convex-hull/down-set equivalence is also essentially correct. The separation argument is valid in finite dimension, and the coordinatewise down-closed property of \(P(\mathcal D)\) is proved adequately. The partition-function inequality follows by writing \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=(1+\lambda)^{-m}Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)\). However, all of these are equivalent forms of the missing theorem, not proofs. #### Shifted reduction The compression reduction is plausible and appears correct. The mass-moving argument in the \((i,j)\)-diamond restores equal \(i,j\)-marginals while moving support back into the original down-set. It could be written a bit more formally, especially the “move mass \(M\)” step, but I do not see a fatal flaw. Still, it only reduces the open partition-function bound to shifted down-sets. #### Capped slice lemma This is explicitly unproved. Since the author says the missing induction would follow from this capped replacement lemma, the absence of its proof is another clear non-readiness point. #### Intersection-theoretic description The claim that no \(r=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) members of \(\mathcal D_w\) cover \([m]\) is correct, because their union has weight \( 637/1000. \] - The pivot-plus-dust limiting inequalities for sample rational \(p\). These checks support the correctness of the local examples, but they do not address the missing proof of Conjecture 1. ### Final assessment The draft contains many correct partial results and useful reductions. The negative direction is complete, and several positive families are proved. But the problem asks for a full classification of \(p\), and the document does not prove the inclusion \[ [0,1/3]\subseteq \mathcal G. \] Instead it explicitly states this as an unproved conjecture. Therefore `answer.tex` is not a complete rigorous solution.

## `answer.tex`

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}
\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{corollary}{Corollary}
\newtheorem{conjecture}{Conjecture}

\newcommand{\Ber}{\operatorname{Bernoulli}}
\newcommand{\conv}{\operatorname{conv}}

\begin{document}

\title{A weighted Bernoulli mean-tail inequality: partial resolution and reductions}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I interpret the question in the universal sense: determine those \(p\in[0,1]\)
for which
\[
        \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p                  \tag{P}
\]
holds for every \(m\ge1\) and every non-negative probability vector
\((w_1,\ldots,w_m)\), with \(v_i\) i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\).

The counterexamples in Proposition~\ref{prop:negative} show that no value
outside
\[
        [0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}
\]
can work.  Conversely, a full classification would follow from the
small-\(p\) assertion (Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}) that all
\(0\le p\le1/3\) work.  That assertion remains the only essential gap in this
draft.  What is proved unconditionally below is the complete negative direction,
several infinite positive families, closure properties, equivalent forms of the
remaining assertion, and a coalescence route that is ruled out by explicit
examples.

\section*{Positive values proved here}

Let \(\mathcal G\) be the set of probabilities for which (P) holds universally.

\begin{proposition}[a coloring criterion]\label{prop:criterion}
Let \(p=a/b\), where \(1\le a\le b\) are integers.  Suppose that every
non-negative \(b\)-tuple \(x_1,\ldots,x_b\) with \(\sum_j x_j=1\) has at least
\[
        \frac{a}{b}\binom ba=\binom{b-1}{a-1}
\]
\(a\)-subsets \(J\subseteq[b]\) satisfying \(\sum_{j\in J}x_j\ge a/b\).  Then
\(a/b\in\mathcal G\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Randomly color each original index \(i\) independently and uniformly with one
of \(b\) colors, and let \(X_j\) be the total weight of color \(j\).  For every
coloring outcome, the hypothesis gives at least \(\binom{b-1}{a-1}\) good
\(a\)-subsets of colors.  Hence, for a uniformly random \(a\)-subset \(J\) of
colors,
\[
       \Pr\!\left[\sum_{j\in J}X_j\ge a/b\right]\ge
       \frac{\binom{b-1}{a-1}}{\binom ba}=\frac ab .
\]
By symmetry the same lower bound holds for any fixed \(a\)-subset \(J\).  For
such a fixed \(J\), membership of an original index in \(J\) is an independent
\(\Ber(a/b)\) event, which is exactly (P).
\end{proof}

The criterion immediately gives the reciprocal points \(p=1/k\), \(k\ge3\):
among \(k\) color classes at least one has weight at least \(1/k\).

It also gives the following second family.

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:pairs}
Let \(b\ge6\), and let \(x_1,\ldots,x_b\ge0\) with \(\sum_i x_i=1\).  Then at
least \(b-1\) unordered pairs \(\{i,j\}\) satisfy
\[
       x_i+x_j\ge \frac2b .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Put \(z_i=bx_i/2\).  Then \(\sum_i z_i=b/2\), and we must find at least
\(b-1\) pairs with \(z_i+z_j\ge1\).

Maintain a remaining set \(R\) of size \(s\) and sum at least \(s/2\).  Let
\(u\) and \(a\) be the smallest and largest elements of \(R\).  Since the
average of the elements of \(R\) is at least \(1/2\), its largest element
satisfies \(a\ge1/2\).  If \(u+a\ge1\), then \(a\) makes good pairs with all
other \(s-1\) elements and we stop.  Otherwise \(u+a<1\).  Let \(h\) be the
number of other elements making a good pair with \(a\).  If \(h\le(s-2)/2\),
then the \(h\) partners of \(a\) are at most \(a\), while the other \(s-1-h\)
elements are \(<1-a\), so
\[
 \sum_{z\in R}z
   < a+ha+(s-1-h)(1-a)
   =s-1-h+a(2h-s+2)\le s/2.
\]
The last inequality uses \(a\ge1/2\) and \(2h-s+2\le0\).  This contradiction
shows that \(h\ge\lfloor s/2\rfloor\).  Count these \(h\) pairs, delete \(u\)
and \(a\), and continue.  The remaining sum is
\(>s/2-1=(s-2)/2\), so the invariant is preserved.  Pairs counted at different
stages are distinct.  If the process reaches two remaining elements, their sum is
at least \(1\) by the invariant, so this final pair is counted and the process
stops; if one element remains, no further pair is available.  These terminal
possibilities are included in the ``never stops early'' count below.

If the procedure never stops before the terminal case, then for \(b=2r\) it
counts
\(r+(r-1)+\cdots+1\ge2r-1=b-1\), and for \(b=2r+1\) it counts
\(r+(r-1)+\cdots+1\ge2r=b-1\), using \(r\ge3\).

If it stops after \(t\) deletion steps, then \(s=b-2t\) elements remain and the
number counted is
\[
       \sum_{i=0}^{t-1}\left\lfloor \frac{b-2i}{2}\right\rfloor+(b-2t-1).
\]
For \(b=2r\) this exceeds \(b-1\) by
\[
       \frac{t(2r-t-3)}{2}\ge0\qquad(0\le t\le r-1,\ r\ge3),
\]
and for \(b=2r+1\) the same displayed expression is the excess over \(b-1\).
Hence at least \(b-1\) good pairs are always counted.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}\label{cor:positive}
The desired inequality holds for \(p=0\), for \(p=1/k\) with every integer
\(k\ge3\), for \(p=2/b\) with every integer \(b\ge6\), for \(p=1/2\), and for
\(p=1\).
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The cases \(p=0,1\) are immediate.  The reciprocal cases follow from
Proposition~\ref{prop:criterion} with \(a=1\), and Lemma~\ref{lem:pairs} plus
Proposition~\ref{prop:criterion} gives \(p=2/b\), \(b\ge6\).

For \(p=1/2\), put \(X=\sum_i w_i v_i\).  Since \(1-v_i\) has the same law as
\(v_i\), \(X\) and \(1-X\) have the same distribution.  Hence
\(\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2]\), and so \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\).
\end{proof}

A possible pointwise verification of the hypothesis of
Proposition~\ref{prop:criterion} would ask that, for every non-negative
\(b\)-tuple of total \(1\), at least \(\binom{b-1}{a-1}\) of the
\(a\)-subsets have load at least \(a/b\).  This deterministic statement is
false already at \(a/b=3/10\): take seven
loads equal to \(1/7\) and three loads equal to \(0\).  A triple is then heavy
only if all three of its points come from the seven positive positions, giving
\(\binom73=35<36=\binom92\).  Thus the missing rational cases require genuinely
averaged information about the random coloring, not just a pointwise
Manickam--Miklos--Singhi type count.

\begin{proposition}[multiplicative closure]\label{prop:closure}
If \(p,q\in\mathcal G\), then \(pq\in\mathcal G\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
If \(pq=0\), the conclusion is trivial.  Otherwise generate independent
\(\Ber(pq)\) variables as \(v_i=x_i y_i\), where
\(x_i\) are i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\), \(y_i\) are i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(q)\), and the two
families are independent.  Put \(T=\sum_i w_i x_i\).  With probability at least
\(p\), \(T\ge p\).  Conditional on such an outcome, the normalized weights
\(w_i x_i/T\) form a probability vector, and the \(q\)-inequality gives
\[
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i \frac{w_i x_i}{T}y_i\ge q\,\middle|\,x_1,\ldots,x_m\right]
 \ge q .
\]
On this event \(\sum_i w_i x_i y_i\ge qT\ge pq\), proving the claim.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[closure of the set of good probabilities]\label{prop:closed}
The set \(\mathcal G\) is closed in \([0,1]\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(p_n\in\mathcal G\) and \(p_n\to p\).  Fix a finite weight vector \(w\).
For \(r\in[0,1]\) put
\[
        F(r)=\Pr_r\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge r\right],
\]
where the subscript denotes the Bernoulli parameter.  There are only finitely
many subset sums \(w(A)\).

If infinitely many \(p_n\le p\), pass to that subsequence.  Among the finitely
many subset sums strictly smaller than \(p\), let \(p-\delta\) be the largest
one, if it exists; otherwise take any \(\delta>0\).  For all large \(n\),
\(p_n>p-\delta\), so no subset sum lies in \([p_n,p)\).  Hence the
event \(\{w(A)\ge p_n\}\) is eventually the same family of subsets as
\(\{w(A)\ge p\}\).  Therefore \(F(p)=\lim F(p_n)\ge\lim p_n=p\).

If instead infinitely many \(p_n>p\), pass to that subsequence.  Let \(p+\delta\)
be the smallest subset sum strictly larger than \(p\), if it exists; otherwise
take any \(\delta>0\).  For all large \(n\), \(p_n<p+\delta\), so the event
\(\{w(A)\ge p_n\}\) is the fixed family \(\{w(A)>p\}\), and
\[
       \mu_p\{A:w(A)>p\}=\lim F(p_n)\ge p .
\]
Since \(\{w(A)>p\}\subseteq\{w(A)\ge p\}\), again \(F(p)\ge p\).  As the weight
vector was arbitrary, \(p\in\mathcal G\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}\label{cor:base}
If the inequality can be proved for all \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), then it holds for
all \(p\in[0,1/3]\).
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
For \(0<p\le1/3\), choose \(n\ge0\) such that \(2^n p\in[1/6,1/3]\).  Since
\(1/2\in\mathcal G\), repeated use of Proposition~\ref{prop:closure} gives
\(p=(2^n p)2^{-n}\in\mathcal G\).  The point \(p=0\) is trivial.
\end{proof}

\section*{Values that cannot work}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:negative}
The desired universal inequality fails for every
\(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
If \(1/3<p<1/2\), take \(m=3\) and \(w_1=w_2=w_3=1/3\).  At least two
successes are needed, so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\frac{v_1+v_2+v_3}{3}\ge p\right]
  =3p^2(1-p)+p^3
  =3p^2-2p^3 .
\]
But \(3p^2-2p^3-p=p(1-p)(2p-1)<0\) on \(1/3<p<1/2\).

If \(1/2<p<1\), take \(m=2\) and \(w_1=w_2=1/2\).  The event requires two
successes and has probability \(p^2<p\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Equivalent forms of the remaining small-\(p\) assertion}

\begin{conjecture}[small-\(p\) assertion]\label{conj:missing}
If \(0\le p\le1/3\), \(w_i\ge0\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and the \(v_i\)'s are
i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\), then
\[
   \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p .
\]
\end{conjecture}

The assertion is sharp even in finite examples: if one weight is at least \(p\) and
the sum of all remaining weights is \(<p\), then the event in (P) is exactly
the event that this large coordinate succeeds, and its probability is \(p\).

The nontrivial sharpness mechanism with all weights below \(p\) is a pivot plus
dust.  Fix \(0<p\le1/3\), choose \(0<\varepsilon<p\), put
\(a=p-\varepsilon\), and take
\[
   w_0=a,\qquad
   w_1=\cdots=w_N=\frac{1-a}{N}.
\]
For all sufficiently large \(N\), the dust weights \((1-a)/N\), as well as the
pivot \(a\), are below \(p\).
If \(Y_N=(1-a)N^{-1}{\rm Bin}(N,p)\), then
\[
 \Pr\!\left[w_0v_0+Y_N\ge p\right]
 =
 p\,\Pr[Y_N\ge\varepsilon]+(1-p)\Pr[Y_N\ge p].
\]
Since \(Y_N\to p(1-a)=p(1-p+\varepsilon)\) in probability and
\(\varepsilon<p(1-p+\varepsilon)<p\), this probability tends to \(p\).

A particularly useful normalization is the following ``unit-gap'' form.

\begin{proposition}[unit-gap form]\label{prop:unitgap}
For a fixed \(0<p\le1/3\), Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is equivalent to the
following assertion.  Let \(q=1-p\), let \(0\le b_i\le1\),
\(B=\sum_i b_i\), and let \(\zeta_i\) be i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\).  Then
\[
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\ge p(B+1)\right]
 \ge
 \frac{p}{q}\,
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i<p(B+1)-1\right].            \tag{UG}
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Assume Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}.  Apply it to the normalized coefficient vector
\((1,b_1,\ldots,b_n)/(B+1)\), and condition on the
Bernoulli variable multiplying the coefficient \(1\).  With
\(S=\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\),
\[
 p\,\Pr[S\ge p(B+1)-1]+q\,\Pr[S\ge p(B+1)]\ge p,
\]
which rearranges to (UG).

Conversely, suppose (UG) is known.  If an original weight is at least \(p\),
then that coordinate alone proves (P).  Otherwise choose a pivot of maximal
weight \(a\in(0,p)\).  Denote the residual weights by \(u_i\), and write
\(u_i=a b_i\).  Then \(0\le b_i\le1\) and \(a(B+1)=1\).  Multiplying (UG) by \(a\) in the thresholds
gives
\[
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i\ge p\right]
 \ge \frac pq
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i<p-a\right].
\]
Conditioning on the pivot variable then yields
\[
 p\,\Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i\ge p-a\right]
 +q\,\Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p,
\]
which is (P).
\end{proof}

The same statement will sometimes be more convenient in the following
homogeneous form.  For every \(h>0\), every \(0\le b_i\le h\), and
\(B=\sum_i b_i\),
\[
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\ge p(B+h)\right]
 \ge
 \frac{p}{q}\,
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i<p(B+h)-h\right].            \tag{HUG}
\]
Indeed, dividing all coefficients by \(h\) reduces (HUG) to (UG), and \(h=1\)
recovers (UG).  Thus Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}, (UG), and (HUG) are
equivalent for each fixed \(0<p\le1/3\), with \(p=0\) trivial.

A final equivalent way to view (HUG) is as a centered two-tail comparison.
After scaling to \(h=1\), put
\[
        Y=\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p),\qquad 0\le b_i\le1 .
\]
Since \(\mathbb E Y=0\), (HUG) at shift \(0\) is exactly
\[
        q\,\Pr[Y\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[Y<-q].                  \tag{CTC}
\]
Thus the missing small-\(p\) theorem is equivalent to (CTC) for every finite
sum of independent two-point mean-zero variables which take the values
\(q b_i\) and \(-p b_i\), with \(0\le b_i\le1\).  This formulation isolates
the problem as a sharp comparison between the first right excursion of size
\(p\) and the first left excursion of size \(q\).

The following shifted version is one rigorous piece of the natural induction.
It does not cover the pivot normalization needed for the full conjecture, but it
identifies exactly where the one-coordinate induction breaks.

\begin{proposition}[subcritical shifted unit-gap inequality]\label{prop:subcritical}
Let \(0<p\le1/3\), \(q=1-p\), \(h>0\), \(0\le b_i\le h\), \(B=\sum_i b_i\),
and \(S=\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\), where the \(\zeta_i\)'s are i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\).
For
\[
        0\le \delta\le qh-pB
\]
one has
\[
 q\,\Pr\!\left[S\ge p(B+h)+\delta\right]
 \ge
 p\,\Pr\!\left[S<p(B+h)+\delta-h\right].                 \tag{6}
\]
In particular (HUG) is proved whenever \(B\le qh/p\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
We induct on the number of coefficients.  If there are none, then
\(B=0\), and for \(0\le\delta\le qh\) both events in (6) are empty (with the
left threshold positive and the right threshold non-positive).

Write \(S=R+c\zeta\), \(B=B'+c\), with \(0\le c\le h\).  Put
\(T=p(B+h)+\delta\).  The difference between the two sides of (6) is
\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
& q\Pr[S\ge T]-p\Pr[S<T-h]                                      \\
&\quad =
q\Big(q\Pr[R\ge T]-p\Pr[R<T-h]\Big)
+p\Big(q\Pr[R\ge T-c]-p\Pr[R<T-h-c]\Big).
\end{aligned}                                                   \tag{7}
\end{equation}
For the first bracket, observe that
\[
        T=p(B'+h)+(\delta+pc),
\]
and
\[
        0\le\delta+pc\le qh-pB+pc=qh-pB'.
\]
Thus the induction hypothesis applies to \(R\) with shift \(\delta+pc\).

For the second bracket,
\[
        T-c=p(B'+h)+(\delta-qc).
\]
If \(\delta-qc\ge0\), then
\[
        \delta-qc\le\delta\le qh-pB\le qh-pB',
\]
so induction applies again.  If \(\delta-qc<0\), then
\(T-c<p(B'+h)\) and \(T-h-c<p(B'+h)-h\).  Hence
\[
 \Pr[R\ge T-c]\ge \Pr[R\ge p(B'+h)]
\]
and
\[
 \Pr[R<T-h-c]\le \Pr[R<p(B'+h)-h].
\]
The induction hypothesis at shift \(0\) therefore makes the second bracket
non-negative.  Both brackets in (7) are non-negative, completing the induction.
\end{proof}

For rational parameters there is also a useful exact cyclic formulation.  The
case \(p=0\) is trivial, so let \(p=r/b\le1/3\) with \(1\le r\le b\), in
lowest or non-lowest terms.  Assign each coefficient \(w_i\)
independently and uniformly to one of the \(b\) cyclic positions
\(\mathbb Z/b\mathbb Z\), and let \(L_j\) be the total load in the cyclic
interval \([j,j+r)\).  Then
\[
        \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i<p\right]
        =\frac1b\,\mathbb E\,\#\{j:L_j<r/b\}.              \tag{8}
\]
Consequently the rational case of Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is equivalent
to the averaged cyclic inequality
\[
        \mathbb E\,\#\{j:L_j<r/b\}\le b-r .                \tag{9}
\]
The corresponding deterministic assertion for each fixed load vector is false.
For \(r/b=2/7\), the cyclic load vector
\[
   \left(\frac14,\frac14,0,\frac14,0,\frac14,0\right)
\]
has six of its seven adjacent 2-intervals of load \(1/4<2/7\), and only the
interval through the first two positions is heavy.  Thus a cyclic proof would
have to use the randomness of the allocation, not just a pointwise interval
count.

There is also a useful sequential identity for \(0<p\le1/3\).  Fix any ordering
of the original weights and put \(S_i=\sum_{j\le i}w_jv_j\), \(S_0=0\).  Since
the increments are non-negative,
\[
 \Pr[S_i<p]=\Pr[S_{i-1}<p]
      -p\,\Pr[p-w_i\le S_{i-1}<p].
\]
Iterating gives
\[
 \Pr[S_m<p]
   =1-p\sum_{i=1}^m\Pr[p-w_i\le S_{i-1}<p].              \tag{10}
\]
Hence the small-\(p\) assertion is equivalent to the crossing-intensity bound
\[
        \sum_{i=1}^m\Pr[p-w_i\le S_{i-1}<p]\ge1
\]
for every deterministic ordering of the weights.

Here are exact reformulations of Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}.

First, after replacing \(v_i\) by \(1-v_i\), it is equivalent to
\[
   q\ge\frac23,\quad \eta_i\stackrel{\rm iid}{\sim}\Ber(q)
   \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
   \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right]\le q ,
   \qquad q=1-p .                                      \tag{1}
\]
The inequality is strict on the left because
\(\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\) is the same as
\(\sum_iw_i(1-v_i)\le1-p\).

Second, it is equivalent to the following two-tail pivot inequality: for every
\(0\le a\le p\), every set of weights \(u_i\ge0\) with
\(\sum_i u_i=1-a\), and i.i.d.\ \(v_i\sim\Ber(p)\),
\[
   \Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i\ge p\right]
   \ge
   \frac{p}{1-p}\,
   \Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i<p-a\right].                 \tag{2}
\]
Indeed, (2) with \(a=0\) gives
\(U\ge\frac{p}{1-p}(1-U)\), hence \(U\ge p\).  Conversely, if
Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is known, add one extra weight \(a\).  Conditioning on
its Bernoulli variable gives
\[
 p\,\Pr[Y\ge p-a]+(1-p)\Pr[Y\ge p]\ge p,
\]
where \(Y=\sum_i u_i v_i\); rearranging gives (2).

Third, let \(\mu_p\) denote \(p\)-biased product measure on \(2^{[m]}\).  The
small-\(p\) assertion is equivalent to the following convex-hull statement: for
every non-empty down-set \(\mathcal D\subseteq 2^{[m]}\),
\[
       \mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p
       \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
       (p,\ldots,p)\in
       \conv\{\mathbf 1_A:\,A\in\mathcal D\}.             \tag{3}
\]
For the forward implication, take
\(\mathcal D=\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\}\).  If (3) supplied a distribution on
\(\mathcal D\) with all coordinate marginals \(p\), then
\[
 p=\sum_i p w_i
   =\mathbb E\sum_{i\in A}w_i
   <p,
\]
a contradiction.

Conversely, suppose (3) fails for a down-set \(\mathcal D\), and put
\(P(\mathcal D)=\conv\{\mathbf1_A:A\in\mathcal D\}\).  This convex set is
coordinatewise down-closed: if \(x\in P(\mathcal D)\) and \(0\le y\le x\),
represent \(x\) as the marginals of a random \(A\in\mathcal D\), then thin each
\(i\in A\) independently with probability \(y_i/x_i\) (with the obvious
convention when \(x_i=0\)).  The resulting random subset is still in
\(\mathcal D\) and has marginals \(y\).  Thus \(P(\mathcal D)\) is disjoint
from the closed upper orthant
\((p,\ldots,p)+\mathbb R_{\ge0}^m\).  Since \(P(\mathcal D)\) is compact and
the two closed convex sets have positive distance, strict separation gives a
non-zero normal vector \(c\).  The vector must have \(c_i\ge0\) for
each \(i\): otherwise the linear functional \(x\mapsto c\cdot x\) would be
unbounded below on the upper orthant
\((p,\ldots,p)+\mathbb R_{\ge0}^m\) and could not separate it from the compact
polytope \(P(\mathcal D)\).  Thus
\[
      \sum_{i\in A}c_i < p\sum_i c_i
      \qquad(A\in\mathcal D)
\]
for some non-zero \(c\ge0\).
After normalizing \(w_i=c_i/\sum_jc_j\), Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} would imply
\[
 \mu_p(\mathcal D)
 \le \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\right]\le1-p,
\]
contradicting \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p\).

Equivalently, with \(\lambda=p/(1-p)\), the remaining down-set statement is
the partition-function bound
\[
 p{\bf 1}\notin P(\mathcal D)
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 \sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\lambda^{|A|}
 \le (1+\lambda)^{m-1},
 \qquad 0\le\lambda\le\frac12 .                         \tag{4}
\]

One can at least reduce (4) to shifted down-sets.  For \(i<j\), the
\((i,j)\)-compression replaces a set \(A\) with
\((A\setminus\{j\})\cup\{i\}\) when \(j\in A\), \(i\notin A\), and the
replacement is not already present.  A down-set is called shifted if it is fixed
by every such compression with \(i<j\).  Compressions keep down-sets down-sets,
and iteration terminates at a shifted down-set because the sum of the elements
of all sets strictly decreases whenever a nontrivial move is made.

\begin{proposition}[shifted reduction]\label{prop:shifted}
If (4) holds for shifted down-sets, then it holds for all down-sets.
More precisely, an \((i,j)\)-compression preserves
\(Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)=\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\lambda^{|A|}\) and cannot destroy
the obstruction:
\[
        p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
        p{\bf1}\notin P(C_{ij}\mathcal D).
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
The preservation of \(Z_\lambda\) is standard, since compression preserves
cardinalities.  We prove the displayed implication contrapositively.  Suppose
\(p{\bf1}\in P(C\mathcal D)\), where \(C\mathcal D=C_{ij}\mathcal D\).  Choose a
probability measure \(\nu\) on \(C\mathcal D\) whose coordinate marginals are all
\(p\).

Fix \(R\subseteq[m]\setminus\{i,j\}\).  The only points of \(C\mathcal D\) which
may fail to lie in \(\mathcal D\) are moved points \(R\cup\{i\}\) for which
\(R\cup\{j\}\in\mathcal D\) but \(R\cup\{i\}\notin\mathcal D\).  Move all
\(\nu\)-mass on such points back to their \(j\)-mates.  If the total moved mass
is \(M\), then all other marginals are unchanged and the \(j\)-marginal exceeds
the \(i\)-marginal by \(2M\).

Before this move, the total \(\nu\)-mass on \(i\)-only points equalled the
total \(\nu\)-mass on \(j\)-only points.  The bad \(i\)-only points contributed
mass \(M\), while every remaining \(j\)-only point in \(C\mathcal D\) has its
\(i\)-only mate in \(\mathcal D\); otherwise it would have been removed by the
compression.  Hence at least \(M\) mass is available on swappable \(j\)-only
points.  Move mass \(M\) from these points to their \(i\)-mates.  The support is
now contained in \(\mathcal D\), all coordinates outside \(\{i,j\}\) were
unchanged, and the \(i,j\)-marginals have been restored to \(p\).  Thus
\(p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal D)\).
\end{proof}

The following slice formulation records the sharpest induction target I have
isolated.  If
\[
 \mathcal D=\mathcal E\cup\{A\cup\{n+1\}:A\in\mathcal F\},\qquad
 \mathcal F\subseteq\mathcal E\subseteq2^{[n]},
\]
then \(Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)=Z_\lambda(\mathcal E)+\lambda Z_\lambda(\mathcal F)\).
For \(c\ge0\), put
\[
 r_{\mathcal F}(c)=\max_{A\in\mathcal F}c(A),\qquad
 \rho^\lambda_{\mathcal E}(c)=
 \max\{c\cdot x:x\in P(\mathcal E),\ 0\le x_i\le\lambda\}.
\]
The condition \(p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\) separates the down-convex set
\(P(\mathcal F)\) from the reflected capped set
\(\{y:\lambda({\bf1}-y)\in P(\mathcal E)\}\).  Thus the missing induction would
follow from the following capped replacement lemma:
\[
 \rho^\lambda_{\mathcal E}(c)+\lambda r_{\mathcal F}(c)<\lambda c([n])
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 Z_\lambda(\mathcal E)+\lambda Z_\lambda(\mathcal F)\le(1+\lambda)^n .
\]
This lemma is not proved here; it is included to make the remaining obstruction
precise.  The uncapped scalar diagonal reach loses information in exactly the
mixed-slice case \(p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal E)\) but \(p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal F)\).

Finally, for \(0<p\le1/3\), the same down-set has a useful
intersection-theoretic description.  If
\(\mathcal D_w=\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\}\) and
\(r=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\), then no \(r\) members of \(\mathcal D_w\) cover
\([m]\), because the weight of their union is \(<rp\le1\).  Equivalently, the
complement family
\[
   \mathcal H_w=\{B:\sum_{i\in B}w_i>1-p\}
\]
is \(r\)-wise intersecting.  Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is therefore the special
weighted-threshold assertion that such a family has \((1-p)\)-biased measure at
most \(1-p\).

\section*{A coalescence identity and a failed route}

The complement form also gives a useful local identity.  Put \(q=1-p\) and,
for a probability vector \(w\), define
\[
      \Phi_q(w)=\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right],
      \qquad \eta_i\stackrel{\rm iid}{\sim}\Ber(q).
\]
Thus Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is equivalent to \(\Phi_q(w)\le q\) for all
\(q\ge2/3\).

If two coordinates of weights \(x\le y\) are replaced by one coordinate of
weight \(x+y\), and \(Z\) denotes the contribution of all other coordinates,
then direct conditioning on \(Z\) gives
\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
&\Phi_q(\ldots,x+y,\ldots)-\Phi_q(\ldots,x,y,\ldots)        \\
&\qquad
 =pq\Big(
       \Pr[q-x-y<Z\le q-y]
       -\Pr[q-x<Z\le q]
     \Big),
\end{aligned}                                      \tag{5}
\end{equation}
with the displayed half-open endpoints corresponding to the strict event
\(\sum w_i\eta_i>q\).

A tempting induction would be to prove that, unless only one coordinate remains,
some pair has non-negative gain in (5).  This is false, even in the capped regime
\(w_i<p\).  For example, take
\[
        p=\frac3{10},\qquad q=\frac7{10},\qquad
        w_1=w_2=w_3=w_4=\frac14 .
\]
Then
\[
   \Phi_q(w)=\Pr[{\rm Bin}(4,q)\ge3]
            =4q^3(1-q)+q^4=\frac{6517}{10000}.
\]
After coalescing any pair the vector is \((1/2,1/4,1/4)\), and the event
\[
        \frac12\eta_0+\frac14\eta_1+\frac14\eta_2>0.7
\]
occurs exactly when the merged coordinate succeeds and at least one of the two
remaining coordinates succeeds.  Hence the new value is
\[
        q\bigl(1-(1-q)^2\bigr)=\frac{637}{1000},
\]
which is smaller.  Thus pairwise coalescence cannot by itself prove
Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}.

The obstruction is not isolated.  If \(n\ge4\),
\(1/n<p<1/(n-1)\), and \(w_i=1/n\), then \(w_i<p\) and
\[
 \Phi_q(w)=q^n+npq^{n-1},
\]
because the \(p\)-biased failure set has weight \(<p\) exactly when it is empty
or a singleton.  Coalescing any pair gives
\[
 \Phi_q(w^{ij})=q^{n-1}+(n-2)pq^{n-2},
\]
so
\[
 \Phi_q(w^{ij})-\Phi_q(w)
   =p q^{\,n-2}\bigl(n-2-(n-1)q\bigr)<0 .
\]
Any successful coalescence proof must therefore use larger or adaptive blocks,
or combine coalescence with a separate treatment of these reciprocal-chamber
configurations.
\section*{Remaining open issues}

The negative direction is complete, and the positive direction is complete for
the values listed in Corollary~\ref{cor:positive}, their finite products under
Proposition~\ref{prop:closure}, and \(p=1/2,1\).  Therefore a full solution
would follow from Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}; combined with
Proposition~\ref{prop:negative}, it would give exactly
\([0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}\).

The essential missing assertion is Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}.  Mathematically, it
is the assertion that every weighted threshold down-set
\(\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\}\) has \(p\)-biased measure at most \(1-p\) when
\(p\le1/3\).  It appears in the document as the unproved implication
\([0,1/3]\subseteq\mathcal G\).  The approaches recorded above reduce it to
any one of the unit-gap inequality (UG), the strict complement inequality (1),
the pivot inequality (2), the convex-hull statement (3), or the
partition-function inequality (4).  Proposition~\ref{prop:closed} shows that it
would also be enough to prove the inequality on any dense subset of
\([0,1/3]\), or on the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\) by
Corollary~\ref{cor:base}.

The pairwise coalescence assertion suggested by formula (5) is not available:
the equal-weight examples in the preceding section show that every pair can have
negative coalescence gain.  A viable coalescence argument would need either block
coalescence or a dichotomy proving that configurations with all pair gains
negative already obey \(\Phi_q(w)\le q\).

After Proposition~\ref{prop:shifted}, the missing partition-function bound may be sought only for shifted down-sets.  The capped slice lemma displayed after (4) is the most focused remaining
inductive target.  Exhaustive enumeration of all down-sets on at most five
coordinates found no violation of the convex-hull statement (3), and the
extremal separated examples were only the expected dictatorship equalities and
lower-measure singleton-cone examples.  These computations are evidence for
(3), but of course not a proof.

The complement form is also adjacent to the hypotenuse case \(t=q\) of the
``bold play'' problem studied by Fokkink--Meester--Pelekis~\cite{FMP}: maximize
\(\Pr(\sum c_i\beta_i\ge t)\) over convex combinations of iid
\(\Ber(q)\) variables.  For a fixed finite weight vector the strict event
\(\sum c_i\beta_i>q\) is the same as \(\sum c_i\beta_i\ge t_*\), where
\(t_*\) is the next attainable subset sum above \(q\).  Their published
results prove several adjacent rectangles and reciprocal boundary points, but
the near-hypotenuse triangles with \(q<t_*\) close to \(q\), needed for all
\(q\ge2/3\), are not derived here from those results.  Frankl-type
intersection theorems~\cite{Frankl} explain some of the surrounding extremal
combinatorics, but they do not presently supply the weighted-threshold biased
measure bound (4).
```

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}

\begin{document}
\section*{Research notes}

\subsection*{Problem interpretation and expected answer}

Universal interpretation: determine all \(p\in[0,1]\) for which
\[
 \Pr\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
holds for every finite nonnegative probability vector \(w\), with independent
\(v_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\).

Current expected answer:
\[
 [0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}.
\]
The intervals outside this set are excluded by equal-weight examples:
\begin{itemize}
\item \(m=3\), \(w_i=1/3\), excludes \(1/3<p<1/2\), since the event needs at
  least two successes and has probability \(3p^2-2p^3<p\).
\item \(m=2\), \(w_i=1/2\), excludes \(1/2<p<1\), since the event needs two
  successes and has probability \(p^2<p\).
\end{itemize}
The points \(p=0,1/2,1\) are elementary.  At \(p=1/2\), \(X\stackrel d=1-X\).

\subsection*{Reciprocal points}

For \(p=1/k\), \(k\ge3\), a complete coloring proof works.  Color each index
uniformly in \(k\) colors.  Let \(W_j\) be the total weight of color \(j\).  Since
\(\sum_j W_j=1\), some \(W_j\ge1/k\).  By symmetry,
\(\Pr[W_1\ge1/k]\ge1/k\), and color \(1\) is an independent Bernoulli\((1/k)\)
selection.  This proves all reciprocal points, in particular \(p=1/3\).

\subsection*{Main missing lemma}

Need prove the finite weighted Bernoulli inequality:
for \(0\le p\le1/3\), \(w_i\ge0\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\),
\[
 \Pr\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
If some \(w_i\ge p\), the event contains \(\{v_i=1\}\), so the result is
immediate.  Thus the nontrivial case has all \(w_i<p\).  Normalize
\[
 a_i=w_i/p,\qquad L=1/p\ge3.
\]
Then \(0\le a_i<1\), \(\sum_i a_i=L\), and \(\xi_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(1/L)\);
the target becomes
\[
 \Pr\left[\sum_i a_i\xi_i\ge1\right]\ge\frac1L .
\]
For integral \(L\) this is the reciprocal coloring argument.  Non-integral
\(L>3\) is the remaining issue.

Complement form: with \(q=1-p\ge2/3\), the theorem is equivalent to
\[
 \Pr\left[\sum_i w_i \eta_i>q\right]\le q,\qquad
 \eta_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(q),
\]
because \(\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\) iff \(\sum_i w_i(1-v_i)\le1-p=q\).
If some \(w_i>q\), the complement event is exactly \(\{\eta_i=1\}\), since
the remaining total weight is \(<1-q\le q\); hence probability \(q\).

\subsection*{Attempted reductions and checks}

\paragraph{Rational-color deterministic strengthening fails.}
For \(p=a/b\), one can generate Bernoulli\((a/b)\) variables by coloring each
coordinate uniformly in \(b\) colors and selecting \(a\) colors.  A tempting
deterministic claim would be: for every nonnegative \(b\)-tuple \(y_1,\ldots,y_b\)
with sum \(1\), at least an \(a/b\) fraction of all \(a\)-subsets have sum at
least \(a/b\).  This is false even with \(a/b\le1/3\): take \(a=3,b=10\),
seven entries \(1/7\) and three entries \(0\).  A 3-subset is good iff it contains
at least three of the seven positive entries, so the fraction is
\[
 \binom73/\binom{10}3=35/120<3/10.
\]
Thus a rational-color proof must exploit randomness of the color weights induced
by the original independent coloring, not an arbitrary deterministic color vector.

\paragraph{Circle interval deterministic strengthening fails.}
Another tempting representation is \(v_i=\mathbf1_{\{U_i\in[t,t+p]\}}\) on the
circle and to try to prove that for every atomic probability measure on the
circle, the set of interval starts \(t\) for which the length-\(p\) arc has
mass at least \(p\) has Lebesgue measure at least \(p\).  Numerical exact checks
show this deterministic statement is false.  Example for \(p=0.3\): atoms near
\(0.2093,0.5049,0.8102,0.9471\) with weights approximately
\(0.2755,0.1601,0.2716,0.2928\) have good-start measure about \(0.2053<0.3\).
Therefore any circle proof must use averaging over the random atom positions in
an essential way.

\paragraph{Splitting/merging is not monotone pointwise.}
Splitting one Bernoulli weight \(c=a+b\) into independent pieces \(a,b\) can
decrease or increase the probability of crossing a fixed threshold, depending on
the rest of the configuration.  Conditional on a rest value that leaves residual
threshold \(r\), old contribution \(c\zeta\) succeeds with probability \(p\) for
\(0<r\le c\); split contribution \(a\xi+b\eta\) has probability \(p^2\), \(p\),
or \(2p-p^2\) according to the location of \(r\).  Example at \(p=0.3\):
\[
 \Pr_{[0.7,0.2,0.1]}(X\ge0.3)=0.363,\qquad
 \Pr_{[0.7,0.1,0.1,0.1]}(X\ge0.3)=0.3189.
\]
Both are above \(p\), but simple monotonicity under splitting is false.

\paragraph{Moment and polynomial lower bounds tried.}
Pointwise polynomial minorants of \(\mathbf1_{\{x\ge p\}}\), e.g.
\(x(x-p)/(1-p)\), give
\[
 \Pr[X\ge p]\ge \frac{\mathbb E[X^2]-p^2}{1-p}=p\sum_i w_i^2,
\]
which is sharp for a single atom but too weak for spread-out weights.  Cantelli
or Bhatia--Davis style variance arguments similarly give only \(p\sum_i w_i^2\).
Chernoff bounds at the mean are trivial.  Concave-transform minorants such as
\(1-((1-x)/(1-p))^\alpha\) give nonnegative bounds but do not reach \(p\) in the
single-atom equality case except in a limiting sense.

\subsection*{Possible future proof directions}

\begin{itemize}
\item Find the correct Kellerer theorem/reference.  The previously cited paper
  ``Zur Existenz analoger Bereiche'' proves existence of analogous regions for
  finite atomless measure families and does not directly state the Bernoulli

  atomless embedding and how the weak threshold is preserved.
\item Look for a theorem on sums \(S=\sum_i a_i\xi_i\) with \(0\le a_i\le1\),
  \(\sum a_i=L\ge3\), \(\xi_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(1/L)\), asserting
  \(\Pr[S\ge1]\ge1/L\).  Search terms: weighted Bernoulli mean-tail inequality,
  probability above the mean for weighted Bernoulli sums, Kellerer inequality,
  Feige/Kleitman small deviation inequalities, p-biased measure of weighted
  halfspaces.
\item Try a minimal-counterexample proof.  The objective is piecewise polynomial
  in weights, with changes only when subset sums cross \(p\).  At a minimizer
  one may be able to move in the simplex until a boundary \(w_i=0\), \(w_i=p\),

\item Try an induction for the stronger two-parameter tail function
```
